course_title: Culture, Identity and Power in the Ancient World

learning_outcomes.description: Students develop a deep understanding of the influences of Greco-Roman and wider Mediterranean cultures on both the ancient and modern worlds, in conjunction with acquiring advanced discipline skills, through working critically, comprehensively, and ethically with diverse types of ancient evidence, and with a range of ancient and modern historical, archaeological and material culture models and methodologies.

Through the critical evaluation of diverse types of ancient evidence, theoretical models, methodologies, and ideas, students test and challenge a number of traditional assumptions, not only about the influence of the Greco-Roman world on the wider Mediterranean and vice-versa, but also about the influence of the Greco-Roman world on the modern world.

Students engage critically with complex cultural and social issues faced by disempowered and/or disenfranchised groups and communities who populated the Mediterranean broadly controlled by Rome from the last century BCE to the 3rd Century CE, and through this knowledge, are encouraged to become culturally aware citizens capable of innovative and positive social change in the modern world.

Students demonstrate discipline-based and transferrable advanced-level research, thinking, data management and analysis, and problem-solving skills to produce high quality oral and written work for different audiences using evidence-based forms of persuasion.

Students are encouraged to become respectful, inspirational leaders by working collaboratively and independently on problems which raise ethical questions about how the history of the ancient and modern worlds has been shaped or recorded, particularly in relation to marginalised ancient social and cultural groups, and indigenous Australian and Torres Strait Islander groups.

Students demonstrate and showcase professional development skills, literacies, and achievements by working collaboratively and independently on a variety of research and in work-integrated learning activities, and by completing authentic assessment tasks to develop transferrable workplace skills.

Alongside developing advanced transferrable workplace skills in digital technologies, students reflect upon the ethical implications of applying different methods of communicating the outcomes of historical, archaeological, and material culture research to different audiences.

assessments.due_date:
27/07/2026 - 26/10/2026

More information about Tutorial Activities is available on Blackboard.

Students must attend a minimum of 5 out of the 10 tutorials or receive a mark of zero. Notes cannot be submitted in lieu of participation without a medical certificate or SAP.
assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Tutorial Workshop Activities

Further information on Tutorial Workshop Activities will be available on Blackboard under the Assessment Tab


Students are assessed on participation in tutorial workshops by completing the set readings, contributing to, and helping to foster discussion based on both these readings and the evidence discussed in class, and by participating in skill building tasks in person.

Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course
assessments.submission_guidelines: In-Class Participation and in-class submission where required
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
16/09/2026 10:00 am

Please note that you will also be required to be in class for the second hour of the lecture for an introductory session on how to set up your eportfolio.
assessments.task_description:
The In-Semester Exam will consist of questions based on the material covered in Weeks 1-7

Further details will be provided on Blackboard

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Exam Papers and Scripts will be submitted to ANCH3570 course staff at the end of the exam
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
Details of all requirements will be provided on Blackboard and will also be discussed in class. All students are required to be available in the second lecture hour in Week 8 following the in-semester exam for a workshop on setting up your e-portfolio. Your e-portfolio will be 2500 words in length, and will contain the following:

Your Professional E-portfolio Showcase must include the following:

  • 'About Me' and 'My Goals' Sections, a CV (CV's are not assessed),
  • An introduction to the Course Culture, Identity and Power in the Ancient World demonstrating to your e-portfolio readers what the course is about and what you have chosen to write about in your portfolio
  • Employability and Peer Feedback Critical Reflections based on the interviews available on Blackboard.
  • Short Research Paper /Ancient Evidence Critical Reflections based on course themes and evidence


Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course

Please Note: A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students are required to provide the url link their Portfolios order for it to be marked for assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.
